title: Robustness and Interpretability
concept: Vision Transformers are highly robust to occlusions and perturbations, and their attention maps provide a natural way to interpret the model's visual reasoning.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How does the receptive field of a ViT compare to a CNN in early layers?

options.text: ViT has a global receptive field immediately, while CNN's receptive field is local and grows slowly.
CNN has a global field immediately, while ViT does not.
Both start with a global field.

exercises.explain: Correct! Self-attention grants ViTs a global perspective in their very first layers.

cards.title: Behind the blindfold
cards.body: If you cover 50% of an image with black squares, a human can still guess what's in the photo. CNNs, however, often get completely confused by these blocks. Vision Transformers handle this 'blindfold' test surprisingly well. Let's see why, and how we can watch them think.

cards.title: Robustness & Interpretation
cards.body: Robustness is a model's ability to maintain accuracy when inputs are noisy, cropped, or blocked (occluded). Interpretability is the ease of understanding how a model makes predictions. ViT's attention weights create natural, human-readable heatmaps showing which patches drove the classification.

cards.title: Connecting clues
cards.body: If a detective is trying to solve a crime and some evidence is missing, they connect the remaining clues. A CNN gets stuck if a key local clue is covered. A ViT uses attention to connect all visible clues across the canvas, easily filling in the blanks.

cards.title: Occlusion tests
cards.body: In research tests, when 50% of image pixels are randomly masked, ViTs retain up to 80% of their accuracy, whereas standard CNNs experience a massive performance drop. This makes ViTs great for real-world scenarios with messy data.

cards.title: Attention is not a perfect explanation
cards.body: While visualizing attention maps is helpful, they don't tell the whole story. An attention head might highlight a background patch simply because it's using it as a reference for normalization, not because the patch contains the target object.

cards.title: Occluded Image Classification
cards.body: How models perform when parts of the image are blocked:

left.text:
Struggles with missing parts
Local features get disrupted
High sensitivity to pixel noise

right.text:
Highly robust to blockages
Connects non-blocked patches
Learns robust global context

exercises.prompt: Why are Vision Transformers more robust to occlusions (partially blocked images) than CNNs?
exercises.explain: ViTs can relate any set of patches together, allowing the model to gather clues from all visible regions even if the middle is covered.

options.text: Self-attention lets tokens bypass blocked areas and connect representation details across remaining visible patches
They automatically reconstruct the missing pixels before classifying
They ignore position embeddings when an image is blocked
They contain fewer weights than CNNs

exercises.prompt: True or false: An attention map shows exactly what the model decided without any secondary routing noise.
exercises.explain: False. While highly interpretable, attention maps can sometimes route background info for structural normalisation rather than semantic categorization.
Attention maps are rendered as heatmaps (or saliency maps) to show which patches the model paid attention to.
exercises.sentence: By visualizing attention weights, we can create representational ___ showing where the model is looking.

exercises.prompt: Which visual output represents an attention-based explanation of a bird classification?
exercises.explain: An attention map highlights the specific patch tokens (beak, feathers) that the classifier queried to make its decision.

options.text: A heatmap highlighting the bird's beak and feathers while leaving the sky dark
A line drawing showing only the contours of the whole frame
A list of bounding box coordinates around the bird

exercises.prompt: If ViTs have advantages in scaling, global attention, and robustness, why haven't they completely replaced CNNs in every single computer vision task?

options.text: Because CNNs remain much faster, consume less memory on small-to-medium datasets, and are easier to deploy on resource-constrained edge hardware.
Because CNNs are the only models that support color images.
Because Transformers cannot perform gradient descent.
Because CNNs have more attention heads.

exercises.explain: Outstanding! CNNs are still the king of lightweight, high-speed, and low-data edge applications. Next: why not combine the strengths of both architectures? Let's check out Hybrid CNN-Transformer designs!
